coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive electrochemiluminescent DNA biosensor based on hydrazide-modified graphene quantum dots and hemin/G-quadruplex DNAzyme
coredata.prism:issueName: Special issue in honor of Chinese Academician Prof. Hong-Yuan Chen for his 80th birthday
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiaoying
Liu, Li
Wang, Zhaoyin
Dai, Zhihui
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), an alternative of conventional luminescent reagents, has been widely used in electrochemiluminescent (ECL) analysis because of its low cost, non-toxicity and ease of preparation. To further improve the ECL signal of GQDs, we herein succeeded in synthesizing hydrazide modified graphene quantum dots (HM-SGQDs). In comparison with GQDs, HM-SGQDs possessed abundant luminol like units, thus greatly enhancing the ECL intensity. On the other hand, luminol involved ECL system typically needed the participation of H2O2 as a coreactant, while hemin/G-quadruplex DNAzyme (hGQ) DNAzyme could efficiently catalyze the decomposition of H2O2. Accordingly, taking p53 gene as a model, a novel ECL DNA biosensor was developed based on HM-SGQDs and hGQ DNAzyme. The experimental results indicated target DNA can be quantified in a linear range from 100fM to 100nM with a detection limit of 66fM (S/N=3). Meanwhile, the discrimination of single-base mismatch was also achieved with the proposed analytical approach, suggesting broad potential applications of nucleic acid-related clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescent biosensor
Hydrazide modified graphene quantum dots
Hemin/G-quadruplex DNAzyme
p53 gene

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of an ECL DNA biosensor
Characterization and ECL behaviors of HM-SGQDs
Specificity and stability of the proposed biosensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China for the project ( 21475062 and 21505077 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of HM-SGQDs. (B) ECL emission curve of (a) GCE/HM-SGQDs, (b) GCE/GQDs with potential scan from 0 to 0.90V in Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.05M, pH9) containing 0.1M KCl and 1mM H2O2.

(A) ECL responses of (a) bare GCE, (b) HM-SGQDs/Nafion, (c) HM-SGQDs/Nafion/probe DNA, (d) HM-SGQDs/Nafion/probe DNA/target DNA, and (e) HM-SGQDs/Nafion/probe DNA/target DNA/hemin-modified GCE with potential scan from 0 to 0.90V in Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.05M, pH9) containing 0.1M KCl and 1mM H2O2. (B) EIS spectra of (a) bare GCE, (b) HM-SGQDs, (c) HM-SGQDs/Nafion, (d) HM-SGQDs/Nafion/probe DNA, (e) HM-SGQDs/Nafion/probe DNA/MEA, (f) HM-SGQDs/Nafion/probe DNA/MEA/target DNA, and (g) HM-SGQDs/Nafion/probe DNA/MEA/target DNA/hemin-modified GCE in KCl solution (100mM) containing a K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 (5.0mM, 1:1) mixture.
Effect of the concentrations of (A) the HM-SGQDs and (B) H2O2 on the ECL response of the DNA biosensor.

(A) ECL signals of the biosensor with different target DNA concentrations (C(DNA)) (from top to bottom: 100fM, 1pM, 10pM, 100pM, 1nM, 10nM, and 100nM, respectively). (B) Linear relationship between ECL intensity and the logarithm of C(DNA). Error bars represented three parallel measurements for each C(DNA).

(A) ECL responses of the DNA biosensor (a) without target DNA, (b) with target DNA, (c) with M1 DNA, (d) with M2 DNA, and (e) with M3 DNA. The concentration of each DNA was 50pM. (B) ECL response under a continuous cyclic potential of the biosensor with 0.5pM target DNA.
Schematic illustration of the ECL DNA biosensor based on HM-SGQDs and hGQ DNAzyme.
Comparison of the performance of several p53 biosensors.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive electrochemiluminescent DNA biosensor based on hydrazide-modified graphene quantum dots and hemin/G-quadruplex DNAzyme

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials and Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Biofunctional Materials, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials and Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Biofunctional Materials
School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), an alternative of conventional luminescent reagents, has been widely used in electrochemiluminescent (ECL) analysis because of its low cost, non-toxicity and ease of preparation. To further improve the ECL signal of GQDs, we herein succeeded in synthesizing hydrazide modified graphene quantum dots (HM-SGQDs). In comparison with GQDs, HM-SGQDs possessed abundant luminol like units, thus greatly enhancing the ECL intensity. On the other hand, luminol involved ECL system typically needed the participation of H2O2 as a coreactant, while hemin/G-quadruplex DNAzyme (hGQ) DNAzyme could efficiently catalyze the decomposition of H2O2. Accordingly, taking p53 gene as a model, a novel ECL DNA biosensor was developed based on HM-SGQDs and hGQ DNAzyme. The experimental results indicated target DNA can be quantified in a linear range from 100fM to 100nM with a detection limit of 66fM (S/N=3). Meanwhile, the discrimination of single-base mismatch was also achieved with the proposed analytical approach, suggesting broad potential applications of nucleic acid-related clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Among various DNA biosensors, electrochemiluminescent (ECL) DNA biosensor is the promising one for its high sensitivity, selectivity, and wide response range [1–3]. Although, many kinds of semiconductor-based quantum dots (QDs) have been found to have unique ECL activity [4–6], QDs, such as CdSe [7], CdTe [8], and PbSe [9], may cause serious health and environmental problems. To solve these problems, non-toxic graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been prepared [10–12]. However, GQDs possess lower ECL signal than conventional luminescent reagents, which greatly limits the applications in analytical detection [13]. Recently, Dong and coworkers synthesized a kind of hydrazide-modified single-layer GQDs (HM-SGQDs) that exhibited enhanced optical properties [14]. Obviously, HM-SGQDs have overwhelming advantages over traditional ECL materials, while several biosensors were built based on HM-SGQDs composite materials [15,16], HM-SGQDs haven't been used in ECL biosensors merely.

Hemin/G-quadruplex (hGQ) DNAzyme has attracted substantial research interest in recent years [17]. As a kind of catalytic nucleic acids, hGQ DNAzyme possesses horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-mimicking activity, and can catalyze H2O2-mediated reaction that is widely used in ECL analytical system [18,19]. However, different from HRP, hGQ DNAzyme can be easily split or formed under the control of nucleic acid hybridization [20,21]. Hybridization is the key strategy for DNA detection, while hGQ DNAzyme may conveniently characterize the sensing event by providing diverse signals based on its HRP-mimicking activity. On a basis of these principles, hGQ DNAzyme is of great value for DNA biosensors [22–24], especially ECL DNA biosensors that employ H2O2 as coreactant [25,26].

In this work, we designed a novel ECL DNA biosensor by combining the enhanced ECL behavior of HM-SGQDs and the catalytic activity of hGQ DNAzyme. Relying on abundant luminol-like units of HM-SGQDs, a large ECL signal was obtained using H2O2 as coreactant. More importantly, the ECL behavior of HM-SGQDs was sensitive to the concentration of H2O2. Since hGQ DNAzyme could efficiently catalyze the decomposition of H2O2, target DNA can thus be determined by manipulating the formation of hGQ DNAzyme (Scheme 1 ). Specifically, probe DNA was firstly immobilized onto HM-SGQDs surface with the help of Nafion. In the absence of target DNA, probe DNA formed a hairpin structure, and guanine (G)-rich sequence in probe DNA was “locked” in a duplex structure. However, in the presence of target DNA, target DNA could hybridize with probe DNA, releasing G-rich sequence from the duplex structure to assemble hGQ DNAzyme. The formed hGQ DNAzyme caused the decomposition of H2O2, leading to the decrease of ECL signal. As a result, target DNA was quantified in a “signal-off” manner sensitively and selectively, indicating great potential of this biosensor in clinical diagnosis.

Vulcan XC-72 carbon black was purchased from Cabot Corporation (USA). Hydrazine hydrate (85.0%) was obtained from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Company. Nafion D-520 dispersion and Tris(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane (Tris) were purchased from Alfa Aesar Company. Hemin and monoethanolamine (MEA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Company. Other reagents were of analytical reagent grade. Tris-HCl buffer (0.01molL−1, pH7.4) was used as the washing solution. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (18.2MΩcm) from a Milli-Q purification system (Bedford, MA).

The DNA strands purified with high-performance liquid chromatography were purchased from Sangon Biological Engineering Technology & Company Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The DNA Sequences used in this work are as follows:
The sequence used in forming hGQ DNAzyme was indicated with italics, and the sequence used in forming duplex structure was indicated with the underline. Mismatched bases were indicated by bold.

Electrochemiluminescent measurements were accomplished using a three-electrode system, with a modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (4mm diameter) as the working electrode, a Pt wire as the counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode (Xi'An Remax Electronic Science &Technology Co. Ltd., Xi'An, China). The ECL measurements were performed in Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.05M, pH9) containing 0.1M KCl and 1mM H2O2, between 0 and 0.9V at a scan rate of 100mVs−1. The emission window was placed in front of the photomultiplier tube, which was biased at 800V. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were carried out with Cary 60 spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained on Fluoromax-4 Spectrofluorometer (USA). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was recorded from 0.1Hz to 100kHz with a signal amplitude of 10mV in KCl solution (100mM) containing a K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 (5.0mM, 1:1) mixture as a redox probe on an Autolab potentiostat/galvanostat PGSTAT302N (Eco Chemie BV, The Netherlands). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was carried out using a Hitachi H-7650 type transmission electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 80kV (Hitachi, Japan). A Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was acquired on Tensor 27 (Bruker, Germany) in the range of 1000–4000cm−1. All experiments were performed at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of an ECL DNA biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The GCE electrode was polished with 1.0, 0.3, and 0.05mm α-Al2O3 powders carefully on chamois leathers and washed with water and ethanol in ultrasonic instrument, then dried under nitrogen. HM-SGQDs were also prepared according to the method reported before [14]. 10μL of HM-SGQDs suspensions were dropped onto the electrode and dried at room temperature for 2h. To immobilize GQDs and reduce the nonspecific adsorption, the electrode surface was then covered with Nafion dispersion. Probe DNA (10μL, 1μM) was dropped onto the GCE at 37°C for 30min, followed by washing to remove non-grafted DNA. Afterward, the probe DNA-modified GCE was immersed in a 1mM MEA solution at 4°C for 2h to block the uncovered surface, followed by washing to remove non-grafted MEA. Then target DNA solution of different concentrations were dropped on the electrode, incubated at 37°C for 1h for complementary base-pairing reactions, followed by washing to remove non-grafted DNA. Finally, we dissolved hemin in 0.03M KOH solution. 10μL solution containing 2.0μM hemin and 0.03M potassium ions were dropped onto the surface of electrode, incubated for 1h (4°C) to form the hGQ DNAzyme.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and ECL behaviors of HM-SGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in the TEM image (Fig. 1A), HM-SGQDs were sphere with an average diameter of about 5nm, and were well dispersed in aqueous solution. ECL-voltage curve of HM-SGQDs were shown in Fig. 1B. When the potential was scanned positively from 0 to 0.90V, an intensive anodic ECL emission was observed with an onset potential of 0.42V (Fig. 1B, curve a). Although most GQDs produced strong cathodic ECL signal [27–29], HM-SGQDs exhibited strong anodic ECL activities. The unique anodic ECL properties of HM-SGQDs should be attributed to the hydrazide groups on the surface of HM-SGQDs. The anodic ECL response of GQDs was also investigated (Fig. 1B, curve b). In comparison with HM-SGQDs, the ECL response of GQDs was negligible.

P53 is a crucial biomarker in early cancer diagnosis and treatment, and the mutational sequences relate frequently to human cancers or tumors [30–33]. Therefore, in this work, p53 gene was chosen as target DNA to investigate the feasibility of the proposed biosensor. If HM-SGQDs was immobilized on GCE surface, an evident ECL intensity was achieved (Fig. 2A, curve b). Because of the impediment to electron transfer by nucleic acids, a slight decrease of ECL intensity occurred after the electrode was treated with probe DNA and target DNA (Fig. 2A, curve c and curve d). Since H2O2 were acted as the coreactant of HM-SGQDs, the ECL intensity was greatly decreased after the formation of hGQ DNAzyme (Fig. 2A, curve e), due to the catalytic consumption of H2O2 by hGQ DNAzyme. EIS was further used to investigate the stepwise assembly process of the DNA biosensor. Bare GCE exhibited the smallest semicircle (Fig. 2B, curve a), indicating the lowest electron-transfer resistance (Ret) towards the redox couple. When HM-SGQDs was coated onto the GCE, Ret increased slightly (Fig. 2B, curve b), suggesting the immobilization of HM-SGQDs onto GCE surface. After the addition of Nafion, Ret further enlarged (Fig. 2B, curve c), which might be attributed to the fact that the negatively charged Nafion repelled the transfer of negatively charged probe (Fe(CN)6 3−/4−). After the modification of probe DNA and hybridization of target DNA, Ret further increased (Fig. 2B, curve d and curve f). Finally, because of the treatment of hemin and K+, hGQ DNAzyme assembled on the surface of GCE, resulting in the increase of Ret (Fig. 2B, curve g). This should ascribe to strong repellent of hGQ DNAzyme against redox species. These changes of Ret verified the fabrication of the ECL DNA biosensor.

To obtain better performance of the ECL sensor, concentrations of HM-SGQDs and its coreactant (H2O2) were firstly optimized. As the concentration of HM-SGQDs increased from 0 to 3mgmL−1, the ECL signal was significantly enlarged (Fig. 3A). Higher concentration of HM-SGQDs could not further enhance the ECL response, suggesting HM-SGQDs at the concentration of 3mgmL−1 was adequate in this ECL analytical system. Similar trend was observed with the alteration of H2O2 concentration from 0 to 2.0mM. Large ECL intensities could be obtained when the concentration was higher than 1mM (Fig. 3B). According to these results, the optimal concentrations of HM-SGQDs and H2O2 were 3mgmL−1 and 1mM, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and stability of the proposed biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of this proposed strategy for DNA detection was investigated by testing the ECL response to other DNA, including single-base mismatched DNA (M1 DNA), two-base mismatched DNA (M2 DNA), and three-base mismatched DNA (M3 DNA). At the same concentration (50pM) of DNA, ECL intensity with target DNA was manifestly smaller than those with M1 DNA, M2 DNA and M3 DNA (Fig. 5A), suggesting the applicability of this ECL DNA biosensor in discrimination of single-base mismatch. These results indicate the high specificity of this proposed ECL strategy for DNA detection. After successive scans for more than 10cycles, no change of ECL intensity was observed (Fig. 5B), indicating satisfactory stability of this biosensor.

In conclusion, we developed a novel ECL DNA biosensor based on high catalytic activity of hGQ DNAzyme and enhanced ECL response of HM-SGQDs. Using the proposed biosensor, p53 gene can be quantified in a wide linear range from 100fM to 100nM with an attractive detection limit of 66fM. This biosensor revealed selectivity in discrimination of single-base mismatch and desirable stability against successive scans. By simply modifying the sequence of probe DNA, we believe other DNA sequence could also be detected by this approach. Therefore, this ECL DNA biosensor may provide widespread applications in clinical nucleic acid analysis in the future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China for the project (21475062 and 21505077).

Split DNA enzyme for visual single nucleotide polymorphism typing

New molecular beacon for p53 gene point mutation and significant potential in serving as the polymerization primer
